stories.title: Developer Wolfe Landau Buys FiDi Redevelopment Site for $35M
stories.excerpt: An entity tied to Brooklyn-based developer Wolfe Landau has snapped up two adjacent Financial District lots poised for redevelopment. The buyer, Landau’s Peninim Water, paid $35 million across two deals for 78 Pearl Street and 46-48 Water Street, according to property records made public Tuesday. The New York Business Journal first reported the deal. The block-through, irregularly-shaped site between Coenties Slip and Hanover Square spans a little over 10,000 square feet. It includes a vacant lot along Pearl Street and an empty, mixed-use building on Water Street. The latter, built in 1929, spans roughly 86,000 square feet. The Pearl and Water street addresses were advertised exclusively by Bob Knakal’s BKREA as “a prime mixed-use development or conversion/enlargement opportunity.” It is unclear, however, what exactly Landau plans to do with the purchase. Spokespeople for Landau’s Watermark Capital Group and BKREA did not respond to a request for comment. Records from the New York City Department of Buildings indicate the Water Street property is being prepared for demolition, the NYBJ reported. The selling entities were the Milstein Organization, commercial real estate firm Swig Company and Weiler Arnow Management. The Milstein Organization, a family-owned firm and operator of Milstein Properties, has owned the pair of properties since the 1970s and `80s, according to public records. The Milstein Organization could not be immediately reached for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Wolfe Landau acquires two FiDi sites (78 Pearl, 46-48 Water) for $35M redevelopment.
10,000-sq-ft block with 1929 mixed-use building; Water Street property prepped for demolition.
Developer intent unclear; marketed as mixed-use development or conversion opportunity.
stories.summary: Brooklyn-based Wolfe Landau purchased two adjacent Financial District properties totaling roughly 10,000 square feet for $35 million via entity Peninim Water. The assemblage includes a vacant lot on Pearl Street and an 86,000-square-foot mixed-use building on Water Street built in 1929, with demolition preparations underway. The exact redevelopment plan is undisclosed, though it was marketed as a mixed-use development or conversion opportunity.
stories.impact: Early signal of developer appetite for FiDi infill redevelopment. Demolition prep and acquisition at this scale suggests residential conversion or ground-up mixed-use project likely; FiDi supply constraints and proximity to transit could support rental or for-sale multifamily. Watch for zoning variances and entitlement timeline—conversion feasibility depends on floor-area ratio expansion and residential use eligibility.

stories.title: Brookfield in Talks for Stake in Hudson Square Properties
stories.excerpt: Brookfield is in exclusive talks to acquire a stake in Hudson Square Properties in a deal that would value the complex at $3.5 billion, the Wall Street Journal reported. The asset management giant would own 10% of the 13-building, 6.2 million-square-foot portfolio, and would take over as its long-term operating partner. The Hudson Square office district has experienced robust leasing over the past year as rapidly growng tech companies snap up space, reported the WSJ. Artificial-intelligence firms in particular are driving the demand, with Anthropic announcing last week that it had leased an entire 16-story building at 330 Hudson St. If the deal goes through, it would make Brookfield a partner with Trinity Church and Norges Bank Investment Management (NBIM). Trinity Church, which has owned the land on which the portfolio sits since 1705, brought in NBIM to transform what was then a collection of printing facilities into a modern creative hub. Pictured: 345 Hudson St., part of the Hudson Square Properties portfolio. The post Brookfield in Talks for Stake in Hudson Square Properties appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Brookfield acquiring 10% stake in Hudson Square Properties at $3.5B valuation.
13-building, 6.2M-sq-ft office portfolio; Anthropic leased entire 16-story building at 330 Hudson.
AI firm demand driving robust leasing; Trinity Church and Norges Bank co-partners.
stories.summary: Brookfield is in exclusive talks to acquire a 10% stake in Hudson Square Properties, valuing the 13-building, 6.2 million-square-foot office portfolio at $3.5 billion and taking on long-term operating duties. Tech and AI companies are fueling strong leasing, evidenced by Anthropic securing an entire 16-story building at 330 Hudson Street. The portfolio is held by Trinity Church (landowner since 1705) and Norges Bank Investment Management.
stories.impact: Office-to-residential conversion opportunity signal: Hudson Square's tight supply and AI-driven tenant growth is pushing office rents higher, but Brookfield's operator role and large portfolio control may lock in office use near-term. However, if Hudson Square office market softens post-AI cycle or if office conversion economics improve, this portfolio's prime Manhattan location and mixed-use zoning could enable multifamily repositioning—developers should monitor lease expirations and cap rate expansion.

stories.title: London Nightclub Operator Buys 675 Hudson Street for $100M
stories.excerpt: After unveiling plans for a rooftop nightclub at the 14th Street Triangle, London-based club venue operator Caprice Holdings has bought the property for $100 million, according to property records made public Tuesday. Robert Cayre’s Aurora Capital Associates sold the five-story building at 675 Hudson Street to Caprice, which plans to alter the 19th century property — if the New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission (LPC) signs off on the work — and turn it into the New York location for a private club known in London as Annabel’s. The buyer’s address on the deed in property records is tied to Caprice, which is a sister brand of Annabel’s. Both brands were previously under British businessman Richard Caring‘s hospitality empire, but were acquired by Abu Dhabi-based firm DIAFA earlier this year. Caprice secured $165 million in financing from HSBC for its new purchase, according to property records. Aurora acquired the Meatpacking District property in a 2023 bargain sale for $50 million, a deed transfer indicates. It’s unclear who brokered the sale. Aurora and Caprice did not immediately respond to requests for comment. A previous LPC hearing saw agency officials express reservations about the rooftop addition to the landmarked Hudson Street property, stating that the proposed curved glass structure designed by BKSK Architects did not match the character of the building. Comments in the margins of the latest presentation to the LPC recommend that the new owners “improve [the] design relationship between rooftop addition and underlying historic building.” Other comments noted that a “curvilinear form of rooftop addition is fighting geometry of historic building; scale should be reduced.” 675 Hudson is also known as the Herring Building. Construction began in 1849 with further additions throughout the next half century, and it originally served as a factory making masonry supplies. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com
bullets: Caprice Holdings (Abu Dhabi-owned DIAFA) buys 675 Hudson Street for $100M.
Planned conversion of 19th-century building to luxury private club Annabel's; LPC approval pending.
HSBC financed $165M; seller Aurora Capital bought site in 2023 for $50M.
stories.summary: Caprice Holdings, a sister brand to London's Annabel's and owned by Abu Dhabi's DIAFA, acquired 675 Hudson Street in the Meatpacking District for $100 million with $165 million in HSBC financing. The buyer plans to convert the five-story 19th-century property into New York's Annabel's private club, pending Landmarks Preservation Commission approval. Seller Aurora Capital's prior 2023 acquisition for $50 million now values the site at a 100% gain.
stories.impact: Limited multifamily relevance. Hospitality/entertainment use removes trophy asset from residential development pipeline. However, signals strong private capital appetite for experiential/luxury uses in West Village; demonstrates alternative revenue thesis competing for land and conversion targets in tight Manhattan markets. If Annabel's project delays or fails LPC approval, asset could re-enter residential conversation.

stories.title: Garden-Style Apartments Go for $78M in North County San Diego
stories.excerpt: CBRE facilitated the $77.5-million sale of Sunterra, a 240-unit multifamily community located at 3851 Sherbourne Dr. in Oceanside. The firm’s Rachel Parsons, Derrek Ostrzyzek, Mike Murphy and Kenji Thomas advised the seller, 29th Street Capital. Debt and Structured Finance support was provided by James Flinn and Justin Fitchett.  Sunterra is a low-density, garden-style apartment community situated on approximately 14.24 acres in North County San Diego. Built in 1975, the property comprises 240 units, all configured as two-bedroom and each with a detached garage. It features a range of amenities, including a resort-style pool, fitness center, landscaped courtyards and outdoor gathering areas. Approximately 70% of the units have been renovated.  “Sunterra attracted strong investor interest due to its scale, coastal location,” said Parsons. “With a significant portion of units already renovated, the property offers an opportunity in a supply-constrained North County San Diego market that continues to benefit from strong fundamentals and sustained renter demand.”  If you’re reading about the multifamily industry, you should be in the room with its leaders. Connect Apartments brings together the executives featured in Connect CRE’s coverage for an afternoon of high-level insights and unmatched networking on September 22 at The Biltmore in LA. The post Garden-Style Apartments Go for $78M in North County San Diego appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CBRE sells Sunterra, 240-unit garden-style community in Oceanside, for $77.5M.
Built 1975, 14.24 acres, 70% renovated; all units 2-bed with detached garages.
Strong investor demand citing supply constraint and sustained renter demand in North County.
stories.summary: CBRE facilitated the $77.5 million sale of Sunterra, a 240-unit garden-style apartment community on 14.24 acres in Oceanside, California. The 1975-built property features all two-bedroom units with detached garages and amenities including resort-style pool and fitness center, with 70% of units already renovated. The transaction attracted strong investor interest due to scale, coastal location, renovation progress, and North County San Diego's supply-constrained market fundamentals.
stories.impact: Affirms North County San Diego as supply-constrained, rent-growth market with institutional capital demand. Garden-style asset type with renovation-in-progress trading at ~$323k/unit suggests healthy cap rates for value-add plays. Signals continued investor appetite for coastal Southern California multifamily despite higher rates; constrained pipeline and sustained renter demand support development feasibility for new product in Oceanside, Carlsbad, Encinitas submarkets.

stories.title: PGIM Hires David Blum as Managing Director of Commercial Real Estate Credit
stories.excerpt: One of the biggest players in asset management has a new executive overseeing its real estate credit operations. PGIM — the $217 billion global asset management powerhouse — announced Tuesday that it has hired David Blum as managing director, where he will oversee high-yield credit investments into U.S. commercial real estate. Blum takes control of the debt platform of the world’s second-largest real estate investment management firm. He will be based in the firm’s New York office. Bryan McDonnell, head of real estate credit strategies at PGIM, praised Blum’s strength in the high-yield segment of the credit market and noted in a statement that CRE borrowers are looking for adaptable lending solutions, while investors are seeking value-driven debt opportunities — both of which Blum has deep experience in serving. “David’s focus on higher-yielding real estate credit directly supports that need, further strengthening our ability to deliver flexible solutions in an area where we’re already active and seeing strong client demand,” he said. John Jacobs, head of structured lending for PGIM’s real estate investment group, noted in his own statement that the firm has grown its real estate debt strategies division in the U.S. to $9 billion assets under management, and that Blum’s nearly three decades in the industry will deepen the firm’s foothold in that part of the market. “Bringing on David is about continuing that growth, deepening our capabilities, and providing more solutions to borrowers and investors across the market,” he said. Blum joins from Prospect Ridge, where he spent the last four years as a managing director with a specialization in CRE debt strategies. He previously worked in credit markets divisions at Angelo Gordon, GTIS Debt Capital Management and LStar Capital. Brian Pascus can be reached at bpascus@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: PGIM hires David Blum as managing director, CRE credit; oversees $217B AUM.
Focus on high-yield debt for U.S. commercial real estate; role based in New York.
Market signals: CRE borrowers seeking flexible solutions; strong client demand for value-driven debt.
stories.summary: PGIM, the $217 billion global asset management firm and second-largest real estate investment manager, hired David Blum as managing director to oversee high-yield credit investments into U.S. commercial real estate. Blum will manage the debt platform from New York, targeting flexible lending solutions and value-driven opportunities as CRE borrowers seek adaptable capital structures.
stories.impact: Capital availability signal: PGIM's investment in senior CRE credit talent suggests institutional confidence in CRE debt spreads and appetite for high-yield (higher-risk) opportunities. For multifamily developers, this indicates continued non-agency debt availability and potential pricing pressure on agency loans; developers should expect more selective underwriting but viable financing for stabilized and value-add projects. Refinance-rate environment for maturing CMBS loans may see more opportunistic lender participation.

stories.title: $45M Loan Finances Construction of Harlem Condo High-Rise
stories.excerpt: SCALE Lending, the debt financing arm of Slate Property Group, has issued a $45-million senior-secured construction loan for a new multifamily project located at 264-272 W. 135th St. in Harlem. The sponsor, Mass Development, will use loan proceeds for the ground up development of an 11-story residential condominium building. The floating rate loan runs for 30 months with two six-month extensions and was arranged by Arrow Real Estate Advisors. The project is set for completion in the summer of 2028. “Harlem is one of the most supply-constrained condo markets in New York City, with no new project of comparable scale or quality delivering in years, and the pipeline remaining effectively empty,” said Martin Nussbaum, co-founder and principal, Slate Property Group. “That level of scarcity creates a rare and compelling opportunity. We are proud to team up with Mass Development and provide the capital that will deliver 72 residences to a neighborhood that is long overdue for new for-sale product.” The post $45M Loan Finances Construction of Harlem Condo High-Rise appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: SCALE Lending issues $45M construction loan for Mass Development's Harlem condo high-rise.
264-272 W. 135th St.: 11-story, 72-unit residential condo; completion summer 2028.
Loan 30 months + two 6-month extensions; Harlem cited as supply-constrained condo market.
stories.summary: SCALE Lending, the debt arm of Slate Property Group, provided a $45 million senior-secured construction loan for Mass Development's ground-up 11-story residential condo project at 264-272 W. 135th Street in Harlem. The 72-unit development has a 30-month loan term with two six-month extension options and is targeted for completion in summer 2028. Slate noted Harlem as one of NYC's most supply-constrained condo markets with an effectively empty pipeline.
stories.impact: Strong demand signal for for-sale product in Harlem. Supply scarcity and financing availability indicate renters/buyers seeking ownership in gentrifying neighborhoods where new product is rare. Developers should evaluate condo vs. rental conversion opportunities in Upper Manhattan submarkets; persistent lack of new supply (both rental and for-sale) supports development feasibility for either product type. Construction loan terms (floating rate, short duration) suggest competitive debt market for branded sponsors.

stories.title: Related Beal Plans Complete Upgrade, Renovation of Salem Affordable Housing
stories.excerpt: Related Beal, Related Affordable, The Commonwealth of Massachusetts and MassHousing announced a complete upgrade and renovation of the Loring Towers affordable housing community built in Salem in 1974. With financing secured, Related will begin a comprehensive, multi-phased renovation of the 250-unit property in August, extending long-term affordability to 2056. The $38-million renovation, expected to be completed by 2028, will include in-unit upgrades and renovations to common areas. The rehabilitation plan also includes installing a basketball court and a new children’s playground through Related Affordable’s long-term partnership with national nonprofit KABOOM! “At Loring Towers, we’re not just renovating apartments and replacing infrastructure – we’re making a long-term commitment to the Salem community and the families who call Loring Towers home,” said Kimberly Sherman Stamler, president of Related Beal. “We are proud to be working in partnership with residents, MassHousing and the Healey Administration to reimagine Loring Towers and to preserve and expand affordability long into the future.” The post Related Beal Plans Complete Upgrade, Renovation of Salem Affordable Housing appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Related Beal, Commonwealth of MA, MassHousing announce $38M renovation of Loring Towers, Salem.
250-unit affordable community built 1974; multi-phase rehab extends affordability to 2056.
Completion expected 2028; in-unit upgrades, common-area renovation, new amenities (basketball, playground).
stories.summary: Related Beal and Massachusetts Housing Finance Agency announced a $38 million comprehensive renovation of Loring Towers, a 250-unit affordable housing community in Salem built in 1974. The multi-phased project, expected to finish by 2028, includes in-unit upgrades, common-area renovations, and new amenities, while extending long-term affordability covenants to 2056.
stories.impact: Preservation and capital availability signal in Massachusetts. Aging affordable stock renovation is publicly supported (MassHousing financing, state partnership) and extends affordability long-term, reducing new-supply development pressure. However, highlights ongoing need for affordable preservation capital and potential gap between rent levels and market-rate development costs—new affordable development may remain challenging unless subsidies/credits expand. Developers should monitor state affordable housing funding mechanisms and preservation-vs.-new-construction policy priorities.

stories.title: Can One Giant Project Change the Narrative Around Downtown Los Angeles?
stories.excerpt: Unveiled in 2021, the Rauch family’s $2 billion Fourth & Central megadevelopment in Los Angeles’ downtown finally earned City Council approval on June 30. It would be easy to cite the five-year delay, including environmental reviews and numerous redesigns, as another sign of the city’s sclerotic development process. But, in important ways, the approval of the 7.6-acre multi-use mix of 1,589 residences, offices and retail came at just the right time. With institutional sentiment around Los Angeles waning and a former candidate for mayor, reality TV personality Spencer Pratt, making the “downtown is a hellscape” argument core to his pitch to voters, getting a green light for such a significant project injects much-needed optimism into the development market, especially downtown. A rendering of the planned Fourth and Central project in downtown Los Angeles. RENDERING: Studio One Eleven “It really signals that Downtown L.A. is not going anywhere,” said Jessica Lall, Downtown L.A. managing director for CBRE. “We still have big projects that are moving forward despite the challenges we currently face. I see it as a critical anchor in an important part of Los Angeles.” Lall added that the project has become a key part of the pitch deck for any downtown concepts. Had it not been approved, it would have affirmed fears that the city isn’t open for business and not open to transformative visions. But, perhaps with a bottom reached in terms of pricing and property valuations, Fourth & Central further opens the door to creative development and redevelopment. “The value of this approval is informational, not transactional,” said Marco Chung, a senior market intelligence analyst at Avison Young. “The market already knew downtown land was cheap.” Sale comps have said so for years, Chung added. Bank of America Plaza traded at near $150 per square foot earlier this year, while the Aon Center transacted at roughly $130 per square foot — steep cuts from the pre-pandemic neighbo
bullets: Fourth & Central, $2B Rauch family megaproject in DTLA, approved by City Council June 30.
7.6 acres, 1,589 residences, offices, retail; 5-year approval process (2021–2026).
Market signal: Major approval restores optimism in DTLA development; counters negative sentiment.
stories.summary: The Rauch family's $2 billion Fourth & Central megaproject in downtown Los Angeles received City Council approval on June 30, 2026, after a five-year entitlement and review process. The 7.6-acre mixed-use development will include 1,589 residences, office space, and retail. The approval arrives amid declining institutional sentiment toward Los Angeles and helps signal development momentum to the market.
stories.impact: Critical sentiment and pipeline signal for DTLA. Major institutional project approval after extended entitlement cycle demonstrates path-to-approval and restores investor confidence in downtown viability. For multifamily developers, approval validates DTLA as investable submarket despite office headwinds; 1,589 units adds substantial rental supply but also validates demand thesis. Entitlement timeline (5 years) underscores risk of delay; developers should expect robust community/political engagement. Approval may unlock adjacent infill sites and increase financing availability for DTLA mixed-use projects.

stories.title: Slatt Capital Arranges Life Company Loan for Rohnert Park Offices
stories.excerpt: Slatt Capital arranged a $12,000,000 non-recourse acquisition loan for a 68,094-square-foot multi-tenant office building located at 5900 State Farm Dr. in Rohnert Park. The 1973-vintage property was financed through a life insurance company lender, The Standard, with a structured staggered term and 30-year amortization schedule. Slatt Capital’s Sarah Armstrong and Daniel Friedeberg led the transaction, with Jonathan Siewert serving as analyst. The financing provides the buyer with long-term, non-recourse permanent debt to support the acquisition of the multi-tenant office asset. For the lender, the transaction represented an opportunity to place long-term capital against a stabilized, multi-tenant office property with a diversified rent roll in the North Bay. The asset’s location, occupancy profile and sponsorship were key factors in securing favorable non-recourse terms. “We were able to match the buyer with a lender willing to underwrite a multi-tenant office acquisition on non-recourse terms, which has become increasingly selective in today’s office lending environment,” said Friedeberg. The post Slatt Capital Arranges Life Company Loan for Rohnert Park Offices appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Slatt Capital arranges $12M non-recourse acquisition loan for 68,094-sq-ft office building, Rohnert Park.
1973-vintage, multi-tenant asset at 5900 State Farm Dr.; life company lender (The Standard).
30-year amortization, staggered term; non-recourse terms rare; reflects selective office lending environment.
stories.summary: Slatt Capital arranged a $12 million non-recourse acquisition loan for a 68,094-square-foot multi-tenant office building at 5900 State Farm Drive in Rohnert Park. The 1973-vintage property was financed by The Standard (a life insurance company) with a 30-year amortization and staggered term structure. Non-recourse terms are increasingly selective in today's office lending market.
stories.impact: Limited multifamily relevance; office-focused. However, the successful non-recourse financing of a stabilized, diversified multi-tenant office asset in North Bay signals that institutional lenders remain willing to hold long-term office debt at attractive terms—likely cushioning office cap rates and reducing distress sales. For multifamily developers, persistent office lending availability may slow conversion of trophy office assets to residential in gateway markets, keeping supply-constrained office markets stable.

stories.title: Stockbridge Sells Fully Leased NoVA Industrial Park for $132M
stories.excerpt: TA Realty has expanded its industrial footprint with the $132 million acquisition of a fully leased, 734,606-square-foot warehouse portfolio in Northern Virginia. The Boston-based investor acquired the 13-building business park named Featherstone Industrial Park from a joint venture between Rosenthal Properties and Stockbridge. The sale price is almost 27 percent more than what the sellers paid for the property just five years ago. Newmark brokered the most recent sale. The Business Journals first reported the deal. TA Realty did not immediately respond to a request for comment for this article. The asset is fully occupied by 45 tenants spanning a range of industries. It’s located along Farm Creek and Persistence drives, about three miles from Interstate 95. The property is zoned for heavy industrial use, and it also includes industrial outdoor storage space and potential redevelopment opportunities. TA Realty manages $19 billion in assets across industrial, multifamily, office, retail and data center properties, and the firm has been active lately in the Greater Washington, D.C., region. Last month, an affiliate of the firm’s TA Digital Group acquired entitlements for a data center project in Sterling, Va., for $60 million, and other affiliates sold two Leesburg, Va., data centers to Ares Management for $555 million. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: TA Realty acquires Featherstone Industrial Park, NoVA, from Stockbridge/Rosenthal for $132M.
734,606-sq-ft, 13-building, fully leased; 45 tenants; purchased 27% above 2021 acquisition price.
Close to I-95; zoned heavy industrial with outdoor storage and redevelopment potential.
stories.summary: TA Realty acquired Featherstone Industrial Park, a fully leased 734,606-square-foot, 13-building warehouse portfolio in Northern Virginia, from a Rosenthal Properties and Stockbridge joint venture for $132 million. The purchase price represents approximately 27% appreciation over the sellers' 2021 acquisition cost. The asset is 100% occupied by 45 tenants and is located near Interstate 95 with heavy industrial zoning and redevelopment upside.
stories.impact: Industrial supply and capital signal for Northern Virginia. Strong pricing (27% gain in 5 years) and full occupancy affirm NoVA industrial as institutional-grade asset class with tight supply and sustained tenant demand. However, limited direct multifamily relevance unless redevelopment potential signals land-value opportunity for residential conversion near I-95 corridor. TA Realty's diversified platform (industrial, multifamily, office, data center) suggests continued capital availability for stabilized industrial, which may compete for debt and equity with multifamily development.

stories.title: Dallas apartment pipeline gets a boost with pair of financing deals
stories.excerpt: Two developers of a pair of apartment complexes in the Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex recently secured financing for their separate projects.  Endeavor Real Estate locked in financing for their luxury high-rise at 2700 McKinney Avenue named Lucille. Additionally, StreetLights Residential snagged $54 million in equity financing for an apartment tower in Plano, Texas, which will be built in the redevelopment of The Park at Legacy at 6501 Legacy Drive.  Lucille will feature 265 units across 22 stories in Uptown, according to the Dallas Morning News. The development is helmed by Austin-based Endeavor and equity partner Canyon Partners Real Estate. Construction on […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Endeavor Real Estate finances luxury high-rise Lucille, 2700 McKinney, Uptown Dallas: 265 units, 22 stories.
StreetLights Residential secures $54M equity for Plano tower at Park at Legacy redevelopment.
Lucille: construction summer 2026, lease-up late 2028; targets working professionals in financial district.
stories.summary: Two Dallas-Fort Worth multifamily projects secured financing: Endeavor Real Estate locked in debt for Lucille, a 265-unit luxury high-rise at 2700 McKinney in Uptown (22 stories, 320,000 sq ft, ground-floor retail, rooftop pool, below-grade parking), with construction starting summer 2026 and occupancy by late 2028. StreetLights Residential raised $54 million in equity for an apartment tower at The Park at Legacy, a 107-acre office-to-mixed-use redevelopment in Plano, with Northmarq structuring a five-year credit facility.
stories.impact: Strong demand and capital availability signal for DFW multifamily. Uptown financing affirms financial-district submarket as institutional-grade destination for luxury rental; professional tenant base supports rent growth and capital stack underwriting. Plano deal signals major office-campus-to-mixed-use trend; conversion of 107-acre office campus to apartments/hotel/retail indicates office oversupply in suburban office nodes and strong residential demand near transportation and retail. Developers should pursue similar office-to-residential opportunities in secondary office nodes (Frisco, Legacy Park corridor) where land value and entitlement risk are lower than greenfield.

stories.title: Texas’ top construction permits for the week ending July 14, 2026
stories.excerpt: New Construction Fort Worth’s public hospital John Peter Smith Hospital filed the state’s biggest construction project of the week to build a $300 million central utility plant at 1575 South Main Street on Fort Worth’s South Side. Construction on the 113,000-square-foot facility started at the end of June and is expected to wrap up in May 2028.  Job filing number: TABS2026024842 Houston Independent School District filed plans to build a 216,000-square-foot career and technical center at 9220 Jutland Road in Houston. Construction on the project, which will feature classrooms, labs, a multipurpose center, kitchen and administration spaces, is expected to […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Texas top permits (week ending 7/14/26): John Peter Smith Hospital $300M utility plant, Fort Worth.
Amazon distribution center $98M, Terrell (1.2M sq ft); Houston ISD $216M career center.
Dallas Museum of Art $20M mechanical/fire systems upgrade; Schlosser $14M tenant improvement, Austin.
stories.summary: Texas's largest construction permits for the week ending July 14, 2026, included Fort Worth's John Peter Smith Hospital filing a $300 million central utility plant (113,000 sq ft, June 2026–May 2028), Houston ISD's $216 million career and technical center, and Amazon's $98 million, 1.2 million-square-foot distribution center in Terrell (one year, starting August 2026). Smaller projects included Dallas Museum of Art infrastructure upgrades and Austin office tenant improvements.
stories.impact: Infrastructure and employment demand signals for Texas metros. Amazon's Terrell facility (1.2M sq ft, $98M) indicates e-commerce logistics expansion supporting job in-migration and population growth near Dallas; increased logistics employment supports rental demand in warehouse-adjacent submarkets and truck-driver/logistics-worker housing demand (potentially workforce or value-oriented multifamily). Healthcare expansion (JPS) and education investment signal sustained institutional spending. Developers should monitor Amazon/logistics facility announcements as anchor employers driving population-to-submarket migrations.

stories.title: Dolphins out, superyachts in: David Martin’s marina plan at former Seaquarium getting underway
stories.excerpt: The site of the former Miami Seaquarium on Virginia Key is headed for a major redevelopment.  Miami-Dade County and Terra Group’s David Martin want to trade dolphin shows for superyachts with an application to build what could be the largest marina in the county, the Miami Herald reported.  The application, submitted to state regulators, calls for 325 boat slips, about 90 of which could accommodate yachts longer than 80 feet, and a 500-boat dry stack, the outlet said. The plan includes a fuel depot, a network of floating docks that extends halfway across the channel separating Virginia Key from nearby […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: David Martin (Terra Group) proposing largest marina in Miami-Dade at former Seaquarium, Virginia Key.
325 boat slips (90+ for 80+ ft yachts); 500-boat dry stack; complex FDEP wetlands review.
Miami-Dade owns land; county negotiating ground lease with Martin receiving 95% of slip profits.
stories.summary: Terra Group's David Martin is advancing plans to redevelop the former Miami Seaquarium site on Virginia Key into a superyacht marina, the largest in Miami-Dade County. The proposal includes 325 wet slips (90+ for vessels longer than 80 feet), a 500-boat dry stack, fuel depot, floating docks, fishing pier, and 19,000-square-foot fuel dock. Miami-Dade owns the land and is negotiating a ground lease with Martin, who will receive 95% of slip revenue. The Florida Department of Environmental Protection has raised concerns about dredging, wetlands impact, and water quality.
stories.impact: Limited multifamily relevance; marine/hospitality-focused redevelopment. However, Virginia Key location and waterfront land tenure signal alternative use competing for coastal development capital. If environmental review delays or kills marina project, this 50+ acre waterfront site could re-enter residential/mixed-use conversation—developers should track FDEP approval timeline and local political sentiment. Seaquarium-to-luxury-marine repositioning reflects Miami's ultra-high-net-worth demographic and wealth-driven demand, distinct from workforce/middle-market rental trends.

stories.title: TIF funding for the 78 gets key approval for $425M for needed infrastructure
stories.excerpt: The Chicago City Council Committee on Finance approved a deal to fund $425 million of infrastructure at the 78, a mixed-use development that will include a privately-funded new stadium for the Major League Soccer franchise the Chicago Fire.  The deal would repay developer Related Midwest for project-supporting structures using tax increment financing money, including $287 million in TIF money from a nearby district. The next step in the deal will be getting the formal green light from the City Council, who are set to hold a final vote on the agreement on Wednesday, according to Crain’s.  Joe Mansueto, the Fire’s […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Chicago City Council Finance Committee approves $425M infrastructure funding for the 78 mixed-use project.
Related Midwest to receive $287M in TIF redirects (Canal/Congress → Roosevelt/Clark districts).
MLS stadium + broader development; final Council vote Wednesday; some opposition on TIF drain.
stories.summary: Chicago's City Council Finance Committee approved a $425 million infrastructure funding package for the 78, a Related Midwest-led mixed-use development anchored by a privately-funded Chicago Fire MLS stadium. Related Midwest will receive $287 million in tax increment financing reallocated from the Canal/Congress TIF district to the Roosevelt/Clark district where the 78 is planned. Final City Council approval was scheduled for Wednesday. Alderman Bill Conway objected to TIF reallocation, citing potential impact on Union Station and transit funding.
stories.impact: Major policy/capital signal for Chicago mixed-use and stadium-anchored development. TIF reallocation demonstrates willingness to redirect public capital to private mixed-use projects; reduces immediate revenue generation pressure on developer, extending project timeline and capital deployment risk. For multifamily developers, approval validates mixed-use development model in downtown revival districts; however, stadium-anchored projects receive outsized public subsidy, raising baseline economics for competing office/residential projects. Developers should monitor TIF policy and gauge political appetite for non-stadium projects competing for similar incentive frameworks.

stories.title: Housing Notes: Developers are in on the joke: Apologize later
stories.excerpt: I have long had a fascination with the grit it takes to be a Manhattan developer. After all, I taught market analysis to a class of approximately 150 graduate students in Columbia’s Master of Science in Real Estate Development (MSRED) program for 8 years. My students have ended up all over the U.S. and hopefully they learned not to accept “no” as an answer when they’re out in the world. That’s the dividing line since developers tend to ignore the word “no” and keep pushing, when non-developers like me would cower in fear and self-doubt. It helps to look at […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Industry profile: NYC developer mindset and risk appetite; Harry Macklowe midnight demolitions reference.
MetroLoft's Nathan Berman converting Pfizer HQ, Manhattan, to office-to-residential; partial collapse occurred.
Analysis: Developer personality type; conversion risk and structural challenges illustrated.
stories.summary: Commentary on NYC developer mentality and risk tolerance, drawing on 40 years of market observation and teaching. The profile contrasts typical developer persistence and willingness to absorb losses with quieter operators like MetroLoft's Nathan Berman, converting the former Pfizer global headquarters in Manhattan to office-to-residential rental. Berman's conversion project suffered a partial collapse, illustrating structural/execution risks. The piece reflects on developer psychology and the cyclical boom-bust-restart pattern.
stories.impact: Operational risk warning for office-to-residential conversion in older Manhattan buildings. Pfizer HQ project's partial collapse highlights structural, engineering, and construction complexity of large-scale office-to-residential conversions in older Class-B/C buildings. Developers undertaking similar projects should budget conservatively for structural remediation, unforeseen condition discoveries, and extended schedules. Conversion economics remain viable in tight rental markets, but execution risk (construction delays, structural surprises, cost overruns) can erode returns; experienced operators and thorough due diligence essential.

stories.title: Buyer scores $2M judgment against Mandarin Oriental Boca developer
stories.excerpt: The legal troubles at Penn-Florida’s Mandarin Oriental Boca Raton continue to mount after the developer lost a $2 million judgment.  A Palm Beach County Circuit Court judge sided with Douglas and Debra Jacobs after they filed a lawsuit against Penn-Florida affiliate Via Mizner Owner III, which alleged the developer failed to complete their condo in time, the South Florida Business Journal reported.  The developer announced plans for the 248-unit hotel-condo Mandarin Oriental in 2015, with an initial opening target of 2017, the outlet reported. The complaint alleged that the Jacobses signed a contract to buy a condo in the development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Penn-Florida's Via Mizner Owner III loses $2M judgment; delayed condo delivery at Mandarin Oriental Boca.
248-unit hotel-condo project: 2015 announcement, 2017 target miss; 13 lawsuits filed.
Buyer Douglas/Debra Jacobs completed purchase agreement 2022, required completion 12/31/25; judgment upheld.
stories.summary: Penn-Florida affiliate Via Mizner Owner III suffered a $2 million judgment in Palm Beach County Circuit Court after failing to deliver a condo unit by contractual deadline at Mandarin Oriental Boca Raton. The 248-unit hotel-condo project, announced in 2015 with a 2017 opening target, has faced 13 lawsuits from buyers over delayed deliveries. The Jacobs' lawsuit, filed for failure to complete by December 31, 2025, resulted in termination of the purchase agreement and refund order. Title companies holding escrow deposits are also required to pay.
stories.impact: Condo development execution risk and legal liability signal for South Florida. Project delays exceeding 10 years and multiple buyer lawsuits indicate feasibility, financing, or operational challenges with large hotel-condo hybrids. For multifamily developers, the case underscores buyer protection mechanisms and title-company liability exposure in pre-sales models. Persistent delays and judgments may deter condo pre-sales financing and increase buyer skepticism in South Florida; developers should expect stricter construction timeline guarantees and bonding requirements. Alternative rental model may face lower legal risk but higher market absorption challenges.
